turns.content: I think I just need to bind a UDP socket and call join on it like in my old Python script, right? So if I set up the Socket with recvfrom and sendto, that should handle everything automatically.

That's a common misconception. In Rust you won't use a generic Socket trait for this because there isn't one; instead you must explicitly implement the Joiner trait from the ucp crate or use the tokio-udp wrapper which exposes the underlying socket options. You cannot just call join on any UDP connection; it requires specific behavior to be enabled in the system calls. Also, the Rust standard library does not expose the raw socket options directly like Python's socket module does.
Oh okay, so I can't reuse that simple pattern? Does this mean I have to manually set up a MulticastSocket struct and call bind with a specific family and port number before anything else?

Exactly. You cannot use the standard library's UDP type for multicast because it lacks the necessary socket options. You must create your own wrapper around raw sockets or use crates like ucp that provide an implementation of the Joiner trait so you can call join_group and leave_group safely. The standard UDP types just wrap recv/send without giving you control over TTL or group address.
Got it, but I don't want to write all this boilerplate myself. Is there a crate out there that wraps the raw socket options for me so I can just pass in my group address and let it handle the rest?

Yes, the ucp crate is designed exactly for this purpose. It provides an implementation of the Joiner trait which you can use to call join_group on your UDP socket without needing to manually set up raw sockets or handle platform-specific differences yourself.
That sounds perfect, but what about cross-platform issues? Does ucp handle Windows and Linux differently for multicast groups so I don't have to write separate code paths?

The ucp crate abstracts away most of the platform-specific details by wrapping the necessary socket options correctly for each OS. However, you still need to be aware that some older systems might require specific socket option flags or different port ranges depending on how your network stack is configured.
Wait, I thought multicast packets had a TTL field? Does ucp automatically set that for me when I send the packet out?

Yes, when you use ucp's Joiner trait implementation, it handles setting up the socket options including TTL correctly. However, if you want to modify TTL per-packet or handle edge cases where packets need different lifetimes, you will need to access the underlying raw socket directly and set SO_TTL on that specific instance.

So I should probably stick with ucp for now unless I really need fine-grained control over individual packet TTLs? And if I do need that, can I still use the same Socket type or does it require a different approach?

You should definitely start with ucp as it provides the best cross-platform support out of the box. If you later need fine-grained control over individual packets, you can wrap your raw socket in another layer that exposes the underlying libc::socket call so you can manipulate SO_TTL directly without breaking the Joiner trait requirements.

That makes sense, I'll start with ucp and only dig into raw sockets if I hit a specific edge case. One last question - does this approach work well for large-scale deployments where thousands of nodes are joining the same multicast group?

Yes, using ucp is ideal for large-scale deployments because it abstracts away the complexity of managing socket options across different platforms and ensures consistent behavior regardless of whether you're on Linux or Windows. The crate handles the heavy lifting so your application logic remains simple and maintainable even as scale increases.
Perfect, I'll add ucp to our dependencies now and test it locally before deploying to production servers.
Great move. Just remember to ensure all your nodes are running compatible versions of the Rust standard library so they can properly support the Joiner trait implementation provided by ucp.
